rows.title: Norwegian Communications Authority Backs DSA Prohibition on Dark Patterns and Manipulative Marketing Techniques
rows.summary:
Nkom has submitted a consultation response supporting the introduction of prohibitions on manipulative digital marketing techniques under the Digital Services Act (DSA), including countdown timers, spin-the-wheel mechanics and subliminal imagery, applicable to all digital platforms operating in Norway regardless of their location.

On 14 October 2025, Nkom published its position ahead of the DSA's implementation into Norwegian law, identifying a range of dark pattern practices that the regulation is intended to prohibit. No fines are attached to this consultation statement. According to the Institute for Advertising and Media Statistics (IRM), Norway's digital advertising market was estimated at approximately 19 billion Norwegian kroner (approximately EUR 1.6 billion) in 2024.

Nkom finds that the practices at issue, including images or videos displayed too quickly for conscious perception, gamification mechanisms such as spin-the-wheel features, time-limited offers with countdown timers creating artificial urgency, and automatic enrolments, constitute manipulative design that limits users' ability to make free and informed choices. Kamilla Sharma, Director of the Market and Services Division at Nkom, states in the authority's published response that such techniques mislead consumers not because of any lack of knowledge on the part of users, but because the technology is advanced, draws on large volumes of personal data, and exploits human vulnerability.

Nkom notes in its consultation response that the DSA also imposes greater responsibility on the largest digital platforms, including Meta, Snapchat, Amazon and Temu, for the manner in which third-party advertisers operate on their platforms. Nkom finds that particular protection is afforded to vulnerable groups, especially minors, and states that behavioural advertising directed at children and young people, which involves platforms collecting data on minors' online behaviour for targeting purposes, is among the practices the regulation addresses. Sharma states that technology exploiting the vulnerabilities of minors conflicts with rights to equal treatment and consumer protection. Nkom's position is that the proposed rules would apply across all digital platforms irrespective of where those platforms are established.

rows.title: Norwegian Communications Authority publishes survey on AI regulation and confirms supervisory role under EU AI Act
rows.summary:
The Norwegian Communications Authority (Nkom) has published survey results finding that 70 per cent of Norwegian adults support strict laws governing the use of artificial intelligence by individuals and companies, as Norway moves to implement the EU AI Act through domestic legislation.

On 1 October 2025, Nkom released the survey findings alongside a statement confirming its designated supervisory responsibility for the practical application of the AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) in Norway. The survey, conducted between 19 and 30 June 2025 among 1,062 persons aged 18 and over, was weighted by gender, age and region to reflect the national population. Nkom notes a margin of error of at least plus or minus five percentage points at a 95 per cent confidence level.

Nkom finds that 69.6 per cent of respondents, rounded to 70 per cent, agreed or strongly agreed with the statement that there are strict laws and rules governing how artificial intelligence can be used by individuals and companies, whilst only 8 per cent disagreed. The authority notes that women expressed support for strict regulation at a rate seven percentage points higher than men. Nkom's divisional director Espen Slette states that the survey reflects public perception of AI as a powerful tool carrying both utility and risk.

Nkom confirms that the consultation deadline for the proposed Norwegian AI Act, implementing the EU AI Act into national law, expired on 1 October 2025. The authority notes that the AI Act applies differentiated requirements depending on the risk category of each AI use case. Nkom further states that, in partnership with AI Norway, it is establishing a regulatory AI sandbox where companies may test new solutions in controlled environments prior to market release.

rows.title: Norwegian Digitalisation Agency Issues Requirement for Cross-Border Digital Service Interoperability Within the EEA
rows.summary:
The Norwegian Digitalisation Agency (Digdir) has issued a requirement, applicable to state bodies and a recommendation for municipalities, that digital services be adapted for cross-border information exchange within the European Economic Area, drawing on the Digitalisation Circular and the Digital Europe Programme.

On 18 February 2025, Digdir updated its formal guidance stipulating that digital services are required, where appropriate, to be adapted to enable information exchange across national borders within the EEA. Digdir states the requirement is sourced in the Digitalisation Circular and is directed at enabling public authorities, businesses and residents to carry out tasks digitally across EEA member states.

Digdir states that the obligation applies as a binding requirement for state-level entities, while for municipalities it is classified as a recommendation. The agency notes that the underlying rationale is to facilitate seamless digital interaction between public bodies, commercial operators and individuals operating within the EEA area. Digdir further references the Digital Europe Programme as a relevant framework supporting cross-border interoperability, indicating that compliance is expected to align with that programme's technical and governance standards.

Digdir identifies cross-border services as a distinct category within its broader data sharing and use architecture, placing this requirement under the heading of transboundary services. The agency's national architecture team is identified as the responsible contact point for implementation guidance, and Digdir notes that the requirement was originally created on 17 December 2021, with the current update reflecting the February 2025 revision. Digdir notes that no specific penalty provisions or enforcement mechanisms are cited in the published guidance, and no articles from a single legislative instrument are referenced inline, as the source instrument is the domestic Digitalisation Circular rather than a numbered EEA regulation.
